content.text: I need a function that can precisely identify all anchor tags in a raw HTML string and provide byte offsets for their content.

This function uses regular expressions to scan a buffered HTML string. It manually calculates precise byte offsets for each found link, yielding tuples containing the href, location indices, and anchor text. ```python def byte_href_anchors(self, chars=False): ''' simple, regex-based extractor of anchor tags, so we can compute BYTE offsets for anchor texts and associate them with their href. Generates tuple(href_string, first_byte, byte_length, anchor_text) ''' input_buffer = self.clean_html if chars: input_buffer = input_buffer.decode('utf8') idx = 0 ## split doc up into pieces that end on an anchor tag parts = input_buffer.split('') assert len(''.join(parts) ) == len(input_buffer) for part in parts: ## try to get an A tag out: m = anchors_re.match(part) if not m: idx += len(part) + 4 continue before = m.group('before') ahref = m.group('ahref') ## increment the index to get line number for the anchor idx += len(before) + len(ahref) first = idx length = len(m.group('anchor')) ## update the index for the next loop # include anchor plus the idx += length + 4 if chars: yield m.group('href').encode('utf8'), first, length, m.group('anchor').encode('utf8') else: yield m.group('href'), first, length, m.group('anchor') assert idx - 4 == len(input_buffer) ``` The code iterates through segments of the buffer, calculating `first` and `length` offsets relative to a starting index. Note that this method relies heavily on regex matching and doesn't use an actual HTML parser.
Does the function handle cases where attributes in the anchor tag span multiple lines?
The current implementation uses simple regex patterns (`anchors_re`) which typically expect tags to be single-line; multi-line attributes would require adjustments within that regex.
Perfect, this explains how the offsets are calculated. Thanks!
